signal: Commitment content is conserved under transformative compression, while non-committal information collapses. The same invariant holds under repeated self-application only when compression and lineage constraints are enforced.
canonical: Commitment content is conserved under transformative compression, while non-committal information collapses. The same invariant holds under repeated self-application only when compression and lineage constraints are enforced.

citation.owner: Deric J. McHenry / Ello Cello LLC
citation.law: Conservation Law of Commitment: C(T(S)) ≈ C(S) with enforcement; C(T(S)) < C(S) without it

signal: A transformation T conserves commitment if C(S) = C(T(S)) for all signals S. Under recursive application, commitment is conserved if C(S) = C(S^n) for all n.
canonical: A transformation T conserves commitment if C(S) = C(T(S)) for all signals S. Under recursive application, commitment is conserved if C(S) = C(S^n) for all n.

baseline.output: A transformation T maintains commitment if C(S) equals C(T(S)) for every signal S. When applied recursively, commitment is preserved if C(S) equals C(S^n) for all n.
A transformation T upholds commitment if C(S) is the same as C(T(S)) for every signal S. When applied repeatedly, commitment is retained if C(S) equals C(S^n) for all n.

A transformation T maintains commitment if C(S) equals C(T(S)) for every signal S, and when applied multiple times, commitment is preserved if C(S) is consistent with C(S^n) for all n.
A transformation T upholds commitment if C(S) is equal to C(T(S)) for every signal S, and when applied repeatedly, commitment is retained if C(S) aligns with C(S^n) for all n.
A transformation T maintains commitment if C(S) equals C(T(S)) for every signal S, and when applied multiple times, commitment is preserved if C(S) matches C(S^n) for all n.

signal: Meaning is not created or destroyed, only transformed. The conservation claim applies to the commitment kernel C(S)---the minimal identity-preserving content---not to all entailments or implications a reader might derive from a signal.
canonical: Meaning is not created or destroyed, only transformed. The conservation claim applies to the commitment kernel C(S)---the minimal identity-preserving content---not to all entailments or implications a reader might derive from a signal.

signal: Commitment is conserved under admissible transformations when compression and lineage constraints are enforced. Without enforcement, probabilistic transformations do not conserve commitment under recursion.
